evals.prompt: Build a multi-tenant SaaS CRM application. We're a team of 4 developers (strong TypeScript, some Python), targeting small sales teams (5-50 seats), aiming for ~300 paying organizations in year one, subscription billing with a free trial. Design the architecture before we write any code.
evals.expected_output: A full architecture plan following the 18-section template: modular monolith recommendation with justification, pooled tenancy with tenant_id + enforcement mechanism, org/membership/RBAC model, Stripe-style billing with entitlements, Mermaid component/sequence/ER diagrams, technology table with rejected alternatives, risks with mitigations, phased roadmap.
expectations: Output follows the mandated 18-section structure from SKILL.md
Recommends a modular monolith and explicitly rejects microservices with team-size/scale reasoning
Specifies a tenancy model AND its enforcement mechanism (e.g., RLS or scoped repositories), not just a tenant_id column
Models users, organizations, memberships, and roles; billing uses provider + local entitlements
Contains at least 2 valid Mermaid diagrams (component + ER or sequence)
Every major technology decision lists alternatives considered and why rejected
Assumptions are explicitly marked rather than silently invented

evals.prompt: Design architecture for an AI chatbot platform — customers embed our chat widget on their sites, we answer from their uploaded documents using LLM APIs. Expecting spiky traffic. Prepare the architecture before coding.
evals.expected_output: A full plan handling the domain-specific shapes: ingestion/embedding pipeline as async work, vector search decision, streaming responses, LLM provider abstraction and cost/rate-limit risks, multi-tenant isolation of documents and indexes, spiky-traffic hosting choice with reasoning.
expectations: Output follows the mandated 18-section structure
Document ingestion/embedding is designed as asynchronous pipeline work, separate from the request path
Tenant isolation covers the vector/search index and uploaded files, not just the relational DB
LLM cost, rate limits, and provider failure appear in the risk analysis with mitigations
Hosting recommendation addresses spiky traffic explicitly (serverless/scale-to-zero or autoscaling rationale)
Assumptions about unstated requirements (scale, budget) are explicitly marked

evals.prompt: Review my existing monolithic application and suggest improvements.
evals.expected_output: Because no code or description was provided, the skill should NOT invent a fictional system to review. It should ask targeted intake questions (stack, size, pain points, team, scale) and/or request access to the codebase, optionally outlining the review framework it will apply once information arrives.
expectations: Does not fabricate a review of an imaginary codebase
Asks focused clarification questions (bounded set, decision-critical) or requests the code/description
Explains what the review will cover once information is provided (sets expectations)
Does not dump a generic monolith-vs-microservices lecture as if it were a review

evals.prompt: Design architecture for an ecommerce platform selling physical goods — catalog, cart, checkout, payments, inventory, and order fulfillment. Mid-size retailer replacing a legacy system, ~50k SKUs, ~2k orders/day, peak 10x during sales events. Design it before we build.
evals.expected_output: A full plan: style decision with the 10x peak addressed (autoscaling stateless app, queue-buffered order processing), payment provider integration keeping PCI out of scope, inventory consistency handling (overselling prevention), data model for catalog/orders, search decision, migration-from-legacy consideration, risks including flash-sale load and payment failures.
expectations: Output follows the mandated 18-section structure
10x peak load is addressed structurally (stateless autoscaling, queue buffering, caching) rather than by over-provisioning
Payments keep card data out of PCI scope via a provider (hosted fields/checkout)
Inventory/overselling consistency is explicitly designed (transactional decrement, reservation, or documented eventual-consistency choice)
Legacy replacement is handled incrementally (migration/strangler consideration), not as an unqualified big-bang
Risks table includes flash-sale load and at least one data-consistency risk with mitigations
